# ESTIMATION WITH SIMULATED DATA: PARAMETER RECOVERY {#sec-simulat}

Before confronting the model with the real world, a simulation exercise was carried out to verify that the estimation algorithm is capable of recovering the true parameters from data generated by the model itself.

1. Fix a vector of true parameters $\Theta^*$.
2. Simulate data with the model evaluated at $\Theta^*$.
3. Apply Bayesian estimation to the simulated data, as if the true parameters were unknown.
4. Check whether the recovered posterior distribution concentrates around $\Theta^*$.

If the estimation is able to recover the true parameters, we can conclude that the procedure is identifiable and well specified.

## The Observable Variables

The @OrsiInformal model incorporates eight independent shocks, which allows up to eight observables to be included without incurring stochastic singularity. Rather than literally replicating the paper's full specification, **five observable variables** are used — exactly the same ones used in the real-data estimation of @sec-real. If recovery is satisfactory with simulated data and five observables, we have direct evidence that the procedure is valid for @sec-real.

| Observable | Formula | Economic Interpretation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| `obs_c` | $\gamma_{pct} + 100\,\Delta \ln c_t$ | Private consumption growth |
| `obs_inv` | $\gamma_{pct} + 100\,\Delta \ln \text{inv}_t$ | Investment growth |
| `obs_Gc` | $\gamma_{pct} + 100\,\Delta \ln G^c_t$ | Corporate tax revenue growth |
| `obs_Gh` | $\gamma_{pct} + 100\,\Delta \ln G^h_t$ | Personal income tax revenue growth |
| `obs_wh` | $\gamma_{pct} + 100\,\Delta \ln((1+\tau^s_t)\,w^m_t\,n^m_t)$ | Formal gross wage bill growth |

: Five observable variables used in the estimation {#tbl-observables-orsi tbl-colwidths="[15,30,55]"}

No further observables are used in this exercise due to computational limitations in estimating the parameters. In future revisions, the set of observables could be expanded so that no additional parameter needs to be fixed, replicating the practice of @OrsiInformal with seven observables. The table below summarizes the five variables used, their definition, and their economic interpretation.

All five are defined as first log-differences scaled by 100 and shifted by $\gamma_{pct}$, guaranteeing stationarity and compatibility with the linearized Kalman filter.

## Parameters {#sec-simulat-params}

The estimation frees **23 parameters** grouped into three blocks: seven structural parameters ($\alpha$, $\delta_k$, $\alpha_u$, $\phi$, $\xi$, $\gamma_{pct}$, $p_{ss}$), eight AR(1) persistences, and eight shock standard deviations. The true values of the data-generating process are set to @OrsiInformal's reference values for the Italian economy.

Seven parameters remain fixed externally and do not take part in the MCMC sampling:

- **$\beta = 0.997$** (discount factor). Calibrated to the real yield on 10-year Spanish sovereign bonds; very weakly identified with the data collected [@SmetsWouters2007].
- **$\gamma_{ss} = 1.00$** (technological growth at steady state). Trend growth is absorbed by $\gamma_{pct}$ in the observation equations; fixing it at 1 simplifies detrending to make the process stationary.
- **$B_0 = 81.74$, $B_1 = 40.87$** (labor disutilities). $B_0$ is recalibrated to satisfy $n^m_{ss} + n^u_{ss} = 0.19$ (19% of available time devoted to work) given the tax rates of the exercise; $B_1$ is fixed at 50% of $B_0$ following @OrsiInformal. With five observables and eight shocks, both parameters are underidentified without additional restrictions.
- **$\sigma = 0.99$** (relative risk aversion). Fixed to be numerically equivalent to $\sigma = 1$, so that the consumption utility function is logarithmic, $\ln(c_t/\Gamma_t)$. This restriction is common in the DSGE literature but constitutes a limitation: @guvenenReconcilingConflictingEvidence2006 documents that conclusions about the dynamics of consumption and hours can be sensitive to whether $\sigma$ is above or below unity, especially when labor is non-separable from consumption utility.
- **$\tau^c_{ss} = 0.40$, $\tau^h_{ss} = 0.35$, $\tau^s_{ss} = 0.20$** (average steady-state tax rates). Calibrated from the original paper for Italy 1982–2006. In the simulated exercise this Italian calibration is consistent: the DGP and the estimation use exactly the same values, so recovery is not contaminated by cross-country discrepancies. The Spanish values and their justification are detailed in @sec-real.
- **$s = 1.70$** (tax penalty). Derived from the Spanish General Tax Law (arts. 191–193): serious infringement (100%) plus late-payment interest ≈ 2.075; conservatively rounded down to 1.70.

## Priors, Posteriors, and Convergence {#sec-simulat-results}

The prior distributions were chosen to be uninformative, with a single family per parameter group and the same hyperparameters for all members of the group. The structural parameters follow $\text{Uniform}$ distributions with ranges that broadly include the true value. The AR(1) persistences follow a $\text{Beta}(1,1)$, equivalent to a completely flat $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ over $(0,1)$. The shock standard deviations follow $\sigma_i^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(2,\, 0.01)$, with a wide tail.

|  | Prior | Post. mean | True | 95% CI | $\hat{R}$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\alpha$ | U(0.40, 0.85) | 0.419 | 0.630 | [0.401, 0.457] | 1.002 |
| $\delta_k$ | U(0.005, 0.10) | 0.028 | 0.030 | [0.017, 0.041] | 1.004 |
| $\alpha_u$ | U(0.40, 0.85) | 0.452 | 0.660 | [0.402, 0.527] | 1.003 |
| $\phi$ | U(0.10, 3.00) | 0.821 | 0.930 | [0.443, 1.435] | 1.004 |
| $\xi$ | U(0.30, 4.00) | 3.635 | 1.600 | [2.859, 3.989] | 1.002 |
| $\gamma_{pct}$ | U(0.02, 0.80) | 0.296 | 0.200 | [0.035, 0.660] | 1.005 |
| $p_{ss}$ | U(0.001, 0.10) | 0.046 | 0.020 | [0.003, 0.097] | 1.002 |
| $\rho_a$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.919 | 0.990 | [0.820, 0.981] | 1.001 |
| $\rho_b$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.834 | 0.930 | [0.726, 0.937] | 1.004 |
| $\rho_c$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.883 | 0.960 | [0.734, 0.993] | 1.007 |
| $\rho_s$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.662 | 0.940 | [0.141, 0.933] | 1.012 |
| $\rho_h$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.951 | 0.990 | [0.880, 0.993] | 1.002 |
| $\rho_{\xi_h}$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.613 | 0.600 | [0.052, 0.983] | 1.001 |
| $\rho_x$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.999 | 0.940 | [0.998, 1.000] | 1.002 |
| $\rho_p$ | $\beta$(1,1) | 0.518 | 0.950 | [0.028, 0.977] | 1.005 |
| $\sigma_a$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.020 | 0.010 | [0.017, 0.022] | 1.001 |
| $\sigma_b$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.037 | 0.010 | [0.026, 0.053] | 1.001 |
| $\sigma_c$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.017 | 0.010 | [0.014, 0.020] | 1.001 |
| $\sigma_s$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.039 | 0.010 | [0.028, 0.050] | 1.001 |
| $\sigma_h$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.022 | 0.020 | [0.020, 0.024] | 1.002 |
| $\sigma_{\xi_h}$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.054 | 0.010 | [0.035, 0.077] | 1.002 |
| $\sigma_x$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.022 | 0.010 | [0.020, 0.024] | 1.004 |
| $\sigma_p$ | IG(2, 0.01) | 0.096 | 0.060 | [0.042, 0.184] | 1.001 |

: Prior distributions and results of the simulated estimation (T = 120). {#tbl-recovery-sim tbl-colwidths="[7,31,13,12,26,8]"}

The estimation runs on T = 120 simulated observations with 3 MCMC chains of 2,000 samples each and 1,000 adaptation iterations. @tbl-recovery-sim reports, for each parameter, the true DGP value, the prior distribution, the posterior mean and 95% credible interval, and the $\hat{R}$ statistic. Convergence is considered acceptable for $\hat{R} < 1.05$. The prior-versus-posterior plots and traceplots by parameter group are found in @sec-annex-sim.

## Discussion {#sec-simulat-disc}

The central result of this exercise is to verify that the estimation and the model are able to recover the true DGP parameters from T = 120 observations with five series. That recovery is good under the same specification applied in @sec-real is a necessary condition for the validity of the real-data results.

Convergence is satisfactory for all parameters ($\hat{R} < 1.05$). The shock standard deviations are well identified, with narrow posteriors and $\hat{R}$ very close to 1. Among the persistences, $\rho_s$ and $\rho_p$ show very wide credible intervals spanning almost the entire $(0,1)$ range: with T = 120 and five observables, social security revenue and the inspection probability do not provide enough dynamic signal to precisely identify these persistences. Among the structural parameters, $\alpha$, $\alpha_u$, and $\xi$ show posteriors shifted away from the true value.

The priors used in this exercise are deliberately uninformative, which departs from standard practice in the DSGE literature. @OrsiInformal use much more concentrated priors, based on previous estimates and economic theory: for example, the labor elasticities $\alpha$ and $\alpha_u$ are usually centered around $0.60–0.65$ with standard deviations on the order of $0.05$, far from the $[0.40,\, 0.85]$ range used here. Allowing the algorithm to explore values of $\alpha$ close to $0.40$ or $0.85$ is economically unrealistic: it would imply that almost all labor is formal or informal, respectively, contradicting all available estimates for southern European economies. That recovery is acceptable even under such lax priors demonstrates the robustness of the procedure; the real-data estimation in @sec-real adopts much more informative priors, in line with the practice in the literature, which should improve the precision of the posteriors.

The corner plots (@sec-annex-sim) confirm that the eight shocks contribute independently to the fluctuations of the observables, with no significant cross-correlations among the $\sigma_i$, consistent with the model's identification.

The simulation exercise therefore shows that the model and the estimation algorithm are able to recover the true parameters with a limited set of observables and a reasonable number of observations, even starting from completely flat priors. This gives confidence in the validity of the results obtained with real data in @sec-real.
